stem:
A 12-day-old phenotypically female infant is brought to the emergency department by her parents with 2 days of poor feeding and vomiting and a noticeable change in alertness this morning. She was born at term, weight 3.4 kg, and discharged on day 2. The newborn screen result is not yet back.

In the resus bay she is lethargic with mottled peripheries and prolonged capillary refill. Her parents describe ambiguous-appearing external genitalia (described in their words as a small lump with what looks like a urinary opening underneath). Bedside glucose is 2.4 mmol/L and capillary blood gas shows pH 7.22, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L, sodium 122 mmol/L, potassium 6.8 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 188 /min, blood pressure 58/32 mmHg, respiratory rate 50 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: lethargic, mottled, capillary refill 4 seconds, sunken fontanelle, virilised external genitalia with phallic enlargement and partial fusion of labial folds, no palpable gonads, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: sodium 122 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 6.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), glucose 2.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.5), bicarbonate 14 mmol/L (22-32), pH 7.22 (7.35-7.45), 17-hydroxyprogesterone sent urgently

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 25 mg bolus then 0.9 percent saline with 10 percent dextrose 10 mL/kg and admit to PICU
options.rationale: This is a salt-losing adrenal crisis in classic salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency). Hydrocortisone (stress dose 25 mg IV for infants under 1 year per AU paediatric endocrine guidance) is given immediately, followed by isotonic saline with dextrose to address hypovolaemia and hypoglycaemia. Fludrocortisone is added once oral intake is established. (Source: APEG congenital adrenal hyperplasia guidelines; RCH CPG Adrenal crisis)

options.text: Intravenous dexamethasone 0.1 mg/kg bolus and arrange transfer to paediatric intensive care once stabilised
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity and would not address the salt-wasting component of this crisis. Hydrocortisone, which has both glucocorticoid and weak mineralocorticoid activity, is the standard. Dexamethasone is also avoided in suspected CAH because it suppresses the 17-OHP result. (Source: APEG congenital adrenal hyperplasia guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 0.5 mL/kg of 10 percent over 10 minutes for the hyperkalaemia then reassess plan
options.rationale: Calcium gluconate is reserved for hyperkalaemic ECG changes; potassium 6.8 mmol/L without ECG features in CAH crisis is treated upstream by hydrocortisone and saline correction. Treating only the potassium misses the underlying steroid deficit and risks deterioration. (Source: APEG congenital adrenal hyperplasia guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous fludrocortisone 0.1 mg single dose and 0.9 percent saline 20 mL/kg bolus then reassess perfusion
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is not available as an intravenous preparation in Australia. Mineralocorticoid replacement is given orally once the infant is feeding; the acute crisis is managed with IV hydrocortisone, which has weak mineralocorticoid activity at stress doses. (Source: APEG congenital adrenal hyperplasia guidelines)

options.text: Send urgent karyotype, serum 17-hydroxyprogesterone and urinary steroid profile before any therapeutic decision
options.rationale: Investigations are sent in parallel with treatment, not before it. Withholding stress hydrocortisone in a haemodynamically compromised infant with biochemistry typical of adrenal crisis is unsafe; treat first, confirm later. (Source: APEG congenital adrenal hyperplasia guidelines)

explanation.summary: A salt-losing adrenal crisis in undiagnosed classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) presents with hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and shock around 1 to 2 weeks of age. Treatment is immediate IV hydrocortisone (stress dose) and isotonic dextrose-saline resuscitation. Diagnostic samples are sent in parallel, not as a prerequisite to treatment.
key_points: Hydrocortisone stress dose under 1 year: 25 mg IV bolus then 25 mg every 6 hours.
Dexamethasone is avoided as it suppresses 17-OHP and lacks mineralocorticoid effect.
Fludrocortisone is oral only; intravenous mineralocorticoid replacement is not available.
Send 17-OHP, electrolytes, glucose, karyotype in parallel with treatment, not before.
explanation.pearls: Treat suspected paediatric salt-losing CAH crisis with IV hydrocortisone (25 mg for infants under 1 year) plus 0.9 percent saline with 10 percent dextrose bolus; never delay for confirmatory bloods, and avoid dexamethasone because it suppresses 17-OHP.

stem:
An 11-year-old girl with new-onset Type 1 diabetes is on the paediatric ward 6 hours into treatment for severe diabetic ketoacidosis. She has received 0.9 percent saline 10 mL/kg over 60 minutes, followed by maintenance plus deficit fluids over 48 hours, and an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour. Initial venous pH was 7.05.

The nurse calls you because the patient has developed a headache that started 30 minutes ago, has become irritable, and her heart rate has fallen from 124 to 78 /min while her blood pressure has risen from 102/64 to 138/86 mmHg. She is rousable but slow to respond. The team has just checked a venous blood gas which shows pH 7.18 and glucose 14 mmol/L. The on-call consultant is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 78 /min, blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), pupils equal and reactive, no focal neurology, well perfused, weight 42 kg
data_table.Investigations: venous pH 7.18 (7.35-7.45), bicarbonate 14 mmol/L (22-32), sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), glucose 14 mmol/L (3.5-7.0 fasting), ketones 3.2 mmol/L (under 0.6)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Add 5 percent dextrose to the maintenance fluid because the glucose has fallen to 14 mmol/L
options.rationale: Adding dextrose to the IV fluid when glucose drops below 14 to 17 mmol/L is part of standard DKA management to allow the insulin infusion to continue clearing ketones. However, it is not the immediate response to cerebral oedema, which is the threat to life now. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Increase intravenous fluid rate by 50 percent to address presumed worsening dehydration
options.rationale: Increasing fluids in established DKA-associated cerebral oedema worsens cerebral interstitial fluid accumulation and raises intracranial pressure. The pathway is to reduce fluids and give hyperosmolar therapy. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion and arrange urgent computed tomography of the head before further therapy
options.rationale: CT can be useful but is not the first step; cerebral oedema is a clinical diagnosis in this context and treatment with hyperosmolar therapy must not wait for imaging. Stopping the insulin infusion is unnecessary and will worsen acidosis. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Give intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg to reduce cerebral oedema and continue current fluids
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has no role in DKA-associated cerebral oedema, which is a non-vasogenic mechanism. The standard hyperosmolar therapy is 3 percent hypertonic saline or mannitol. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce intravenous fluid rate by one-third and give intravenous 3 percent hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 15 minutes
options.rationale: The triad of headache, falling heart rate, and rising blood pressure during DKA treatment is cerebral oedema (Cushing response). Immediate management per ISPAD and BSPED guidance is to reduce IV fluid rate by one-third and give hyperosmolar therapy: 3 percent hypertonic saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg over 10 to 15 minutes, or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg. Urgent CT after stabilisation is appropriate but treatment is not delayed for imaging. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guidelines; RCH CPG DKA)

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is the most serious complication of paediatric DKA, presenting with headache, altered consciousness, and the Cushing response (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration). Treatment is immediate: reduce IV fluid rate by one-third and give 3 percent hypertonic saline (2.5 to 5 mL/kg over 10 to 15 minutes) or mannitol (0.5 to 1 g/kg). Treatment must not wait for imaging.
key_points: Triad of bradycardia, hypertension, and altered consciousness in DKA = cerebral oedema.
First step: reduce fluid rate by one-third and give hyperosmolar therapy.
Hypertonic saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg of 3 percent solution OR mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg.
Imaging follows stabilisation, not before.
explanation.pearls: DKA cerebral oedema is signalled by headache plus Cushing response and is treated immediately with one-third reduction in IV fluids plus 3 percent hypertonic saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg over 10 to 15 minutes per ISPAD; do not delay for CT.

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with 6 hours of fever, vomiting, and a rapidly spreading rash. His parents say he was well at school yesterday. On the way in he became unusually drowsy.

In the resus bay he is mottled with cold peripheries, capillary refill 5 seconds, and a non-blanching purpuric rash over his trunk and legs which is visibly spreading. He is rousable but irritable. The team has obtained intraosseous access. Bedside glucose is 4.2 mmol/L. The on-call anaesthetist has been called.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 178 /min, blood pressure 78/42 mmHg, respiratory rate 38 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 39.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: mottled, cold peripheries, capillary refill 5 seconds, non-blanching purpuric rash over trunk and limbs, drowsy but rousable, GCS 12, neck supple, no photophobia documented
data_table.Investigations: intraosseous access established, blood cultures and full blood count drawn, lactate 4.8 mmol/L (under 2)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Defer antibiotic administration and perform a lumbar puncture immediately for CSF cell count and microscopy
options.rationale: Performing LP in shock, raised ICP, coagulopathy, or with a spreading purpuric rash is unsafe. Antibiotics within the first hour are the priority; CSF can still yield useful diagnostic information when sampled later, and PCR retains sensitivity for some time. (Source: RCH CPG Meningitis paediatric)

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg through the intraosseous line with 20 mL/kg saline bolus and defer LP
options.rationale: RCH paediatric sepsis pathway places antibiotics within the first hour after blood cultures, regardless of LP status. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, raised intracranial pressure, coagulopathy, or progressive purpuric rash. Antibiotics are not delayed pending LP. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis paediatric; RCH CPG Meningitis paediatric)

options.text: Administer benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg intramuscularly through the deltoid prior to attempting intravenous access
options.rationale: IM benzylpenicillin is the field option when IV or IO access cannot be obtained. The team here has IO access available, which permits immediate IV-equivalent administration of broad-spectrum ceftriaxone. (Source: RCH CPG Meningitis paediatric)

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 1 microgram/kg bolus to raise the blood pressure before any antibiotic dose
options.rationale: Inotropes are reserved for fluid-refractory shock after 40 to 60 mL/kg of fluid resuscitation; first-line is volume and antibiotics. Starting a bolus of adrenaline before adequate fluid loading is not in the sepsis pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis paediatric)

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg before the first dose of empirical antibiotic for meningitis
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is not routinely given for suspected meningococcal sepsis; it has a role in confirmed pneumococcal or Haemophilus meningitis if started with or just before the first dose of antibiotic. Use here delays antibiotic administration. (Source: RCH CPG Meningitis paediatric)

explanation.summary: Suspected meningococcal sepsis with shock and a spreading purpuric rash mandates immediate antibiotics within the first hour after blood cultures and IV or IO access, plus a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, raised intracranial pressure, coagulopathy, or progressive purpuric rash. LP is deferred until the child is haemodynamically stable. IM benzylpenicillin is reserved for the field when IV or IO access is not available.
key_points: Antibiotics within the first hour of recognition, after cultures.
LP contraindications: shock, raised ICP, coagulopathy, progressive purpuric rash.
Fluid resuscitation: 20 mL/kg crystalloid, repeat to 60 mL/kg before inotropes.
IM benzylpenicillin only when IV or IO access is not available.
explanation.pearls: Give IV or IO ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg in the first hour of suspected meningococcal sepsis and defer LP until the child is haemodynamically stable; never delay antibiotics for CSF sampling.

stem:
A 19-day-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents who measured an axillary temperature of 38.2 degrees Celsius this evening. He has been feeding less in the last 12 hours and seems quieter than usual. He was born at term by uncomplicated vaginal delivery; mother had no maternal GBS swab documented in the discharge summary, and there was prolonged rupture of membranes at 19 hours.

In triage he is alert but quiet, with normal capillary refill, anterior fontanelle flat, and no rash. Fully exposed examination is unremarkable. The most senior available decision-maker is the paediatric registrar on call.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 156 /min, blood pressure 78/48 mmHg, respiratory rate 48 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 38.2 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert but quiet, flat anterior fontanelle, no rash, no respiratory distress, abdomen soft, no focal source on full exposed examination
data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.36 lactate 1.4 mmol/L, capillary blood glucose 4.8 mmol/L, urinalysis pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture and commence intravenous ceftriaxone empirically
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in the neonatal period (first 28 days) because it displaces bilirubin from albumin and can cause kernicterus. Cefotaxime is preferred. Amoxicillin is required to cover Listeria. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture and commence intravenous amoxicillin plus cefotaxime empirically
options.rationale: Any febrile infant under 28 days requires a full septic workup including blood culture, urine culture, and CSF examination, followed by empirical IV antibiotic cover for the neonatal pathogen spectrum. AU guidance pairs amoxicillin (for Listeria and group B Streptococcus) with cefotaxime (Gram-negative cover). Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates due to bilirubin displacement risk. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile infant; Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Perform a full septic workup excluding lumbar puncture and observe on the ward for 24 hours before deciding on antibiotics
options.rationale: Withholding LP and antibiotics in any febrile infant under 28 days is unsafe. The neonatal immune system cannot reliably localise infection, and bacterial meningitis has a high mortality without prompt treatment. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile infant)

options.text: Take blood and urine cultures only and commence oral cefalexin once the urine result is back
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for empirical neonatal sepsis cover and the spectrum of cefalexin does not cover Listeria. CSF must be examined. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile infant)

options.text: Apply paracetamol and observe for 4 hours; if temperature settles, discharge with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Antipyretic response is not a reliable rule-out for serious bacterial infection in any age group, and especially not in infants under 28 days. The septic workup proceeds regardless of fever response. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile infant)

explanation.summary: Any febrile infant under 28 days has a full septic workup (blood culture, urine culture, lumbar puncture) and empirical IV antibiotics covering the neonatal spectrum. AU guidance pairs amoxicillin (for Listeria and group B Streptococcus) with cefotaxime (for Gram-negative cover). Ceftriaxone is avoided in the first 28 days of life because it displaces bilirubin from albumin and can cause kernicterus. Antipyretic response is not a reliable rule-out for serious bacterial infection.
key_points: Full septic workup is mandatory for any febrile infant under 28 days.
Empirical antibiotics: IV amoxicillin plus IV cefotaxime.
Ceftriaxone is contraindicated under 28 days (bilirubin displacement, kernicterus risk).
Antipyretic response does not rule out serious bacterial infection.
explanation.pearls: Febrile infant under 28 days gets full septic workup including LP plus empirical IV amoxicillin and cefotaxime; ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates because of kernicterus risk from bilirubin displacement.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy with a known peanut allergy was given intramuscular adrenaline 300 micrograms by his mother 30 minutes ago after he developed widespread urticaria and stridor at a friend's house following biscuit ingestion. In the ambulance he received a second IM adrenaline 300 micrograms because his stridor recurred 12 minutes after the first dose.

On arrival in the emergency department he is alert, asymptomatic, with no rash, no stridor, no wheeze, and normal vital signs. His parents ask whether he can be discharged now that he looks well. He has had three previous mild anaphylactic episodes and has an ASCIA action plan; he weighs 22 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 102 /min, blood pressure 104/68 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert and active, skin clear with no urticaria, oropharynx clear, chest auscultation clear, no abdominal tenderness, normal mental state
data_table.Investigations: clinical diagnosis, no further investigations indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge now with a clear written action plan and a replacement adrenaline autoinjector to take home
options.rationale: Same-day discharge before completing 4 hours of post-adrenaline observation does not allow for detection of a biphasic reaction. The risk is highest in patients who needed more than one dose, which applies here. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Observe in the emergency department for at least 4 hours from the last adrenaline dose before discharge planning
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends a minimum 4 hours of observation after the last adrenaline dose for any anaphylaxis. He received two doses (the second 18 minutes ago), so the clock restarts from the second dose. Biphasic reactions occur in around 5 percent of cases, typically within 6 to 8 hours. Children who received 2 or more adrenaline doses are at higher risk and may warrant longer observation. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Admit to the paediatric ward for 24 hours of observation regardless of the symptom-free interval
options.rationale: Routine 24-hour admission is not required for anaphylaxis. A minimum 4-hour observation after the last adrenaline dose, with longer for high-risk features (two or more doses, severe asthma, severe initial reaction, remote location), is the AU standard. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Discharge with a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg to prevent a biphasic reaction
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions and are not recommended as monotherapy for prevention. The mainstay is observation and a clear emergency action plan. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Discharge with oral antihistamine cetirizine 5 mg twice daily for 5 days and review by general practitioner
options.rationale: Antihistamines treat skin symptoms only and have no role in preventing or treating airway or cardiovascular components of anaphylaxis. Their use as monotherapy for discharge planning understates the risk. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

explanation.summary: ASCIA recommends a minimum of 4 hours of observation after the last adrenaline dose for any anaphylaxis, with longer observation when there are risk factors for biphasic reaction. Risk factors include needing two or more adrenaline doses, severe asthma, and severe initial reaction. Biphasic reactions occur in around 5 percent of cases, typically within 6 to 8 hours of the initial reaction. Corticosteroids and antihistamines do not prevent biphasic reactions; observation and a clear action plan with adrenaline autoinjector prescription do.
key_points: Minimum 4 hours observation from the last adrenaline dose.
Risk factors for biphasic reaction: 2 or more adrenaline doses, severe asthma, severe initial reaction.
Biphasic reactions occur in around 5 percent, typically within 6 to 8 hours.
Steroids and antihistamines do not prevent biphasic anaphylaxis.
explanation.pearls: After paediatric anaphylaxis requiring two adrenaline doses, observe for at least 4 hours from the last dose per ASCIA; biphasic reactions occur in around 5 percent and steroids do not prevent them.

sources.label: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

stem:
A 7-day-old term boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents who say he stopped feeding well 12 hours ago and is now floppy and cold. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery with no antenatal concerns; the discharge ward midwife discharged him on day 2 with no echocardiogram performed.

In the resus bay he is grey, lethargic, and tachypnoeic. Pre-ductal SpO2 on the right hand is 96 percent and post-ductal SpO2 on the foot is 84 percent. Femoral pulses are not palpable while brachial pulses are present. Four-limb blood pressures show right upper limb 92/58 mmHg and right lower limb 56/34 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 184 /min, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg right arm and 56/34 mmHg right leg, respiratory rate 64 /min, SpO2 96% right hand and 84% right foot, temperature 36.0 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: grey lethargic infant, capillary refill 4 seconds, absent femoral pulses bilaterally, brachial pulses present, tachypnoea with mild recessions, harsh systolic murmur left infraclavicular area
data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.18 lactate 5.4 mmol/L, sodium 137 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.6 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urea 6.8 mmol/L, creatinine 64 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous furosemide 1 mg/kg for the tachypnoea attributed to congestive heart failure secondary
options.rationale: Diuresis without re-establishing ductal flow worsens the obstructive picture. PGE1 first, then supportive care including selective diuresis once the duct is patent and pulmonary congestion is documented. (Source: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Give 20 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline bolus and reassess perfusion before initiating any other targeted intervention
options.rationale: A standard 20 mL/kg fluid bolus in undifferentiated neonatal shock can precipitate decompensation if the underlying lesion is left-sided obstruction with a closing duct. Smaller volumes (10 mL/kg) given cautiously plus immediate PGE1 are the AU paediatric cardiology pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Intubate and commence inotropes with a peripheral adrenaline infusion before organising interhospital transfer
options.rationale: Inotropes without restoring ductal flow do not address the obstruction at the aortic isthmus. PGE1 is the upstream intervention; inotropes are an adjunct after ductal flow is re-established. (Source: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg empirically for presumed late-onset neonatal bacterial sepsis cover
options.rationale: While sepsis is a reasonable differential, the four-limb blood pressure gradient and pre versus post-ductal saturation difference strongly point to a duct-dependent cardiac lesion. Antibiotics are appropriate as adjunct cover after PGE1 is started. Ceftriaxone is also relatively contraindicated under 28 days. (Source: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Commence intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion 5 nanograms/kg/min and prepare for retrieval to a cardiac centre
options.rationale: Pre- versus post-ductal SpO2 differential and the four-limb blood pressure gradient indicate duct-dependent coarctation of the aorta. The patent ductus arteriosus is supplying flow to the lower body; once it closes the infant deteriorates rapidly. PGE1 reopens or maintains ductal patency at 5 to 50 ng/kg/min while retrieval is organised to a paediatric cardiac centre. (Source: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease

explanation.summary: Differential SpO2 (pre-ductal higher than post-ductal) and a four-limb BP gradient (upper limb higher than lower limb) in a collapsing neonate at 5 to 10 days of life indicate duct-dependent coarctation. PGE1 infusion 5 to 50 ng/kg/min is the immediate intervention to reopen or maintain ductal patency. Antibiotics for possible sepsis can be given in parallel but are not the upstream therapy.
key_points: Pre versus post-ductal SpO2 difference indicates a duct-dependent right-to-left or systemic-supply lesion.
PGE1 dose: 5 ng/kg/min, titrated up to 50 ng/kg/min if no response.
Apnoea is a common side effect; have intubation equipment ready.
20 mL/kg bolus is too much for closing-duct shock; use 10 mL/kg if needed.
explanation.pearls: Collapsing 1-week-old with absent femorals, pre versus post-ductal SpO2 gradient, and four-limb BP gradient = duct-dependent coarctation; commence PGE1 5 ng/kg/min and arrange retrieval to a paediatric cardiac centre.

sources.label: RCH CPG Duct-dependent congenital heart disease
